comments.text: Starting with the ideas of Tao in this thread, I put together a write-up that, conditional on abc, settles f(n) between the bounds: n - O(log n) $\leq$ f(n) $\leq$ n - 2 for all sufficiently large n. The writeup is at this Overleaf link. I tried to push this to an abc-unconditional proof, but every reduction I attempted and every sub-case I treated still left a remaining kernel that looks a lot like abc itself: typically a primitive triple of nearby integers with anomalously small radical relative to height, or an arithmetic configuration that morally invokes the same bound. In this sense, the problem is settled at least partially for now, and the abc-unconditional question remains hard. The proof was developed via an automated multi-turn audit-and-revise scaffold that I built, which iteratively queried ChatGPT-5.5-Pro, running for tens of consecutive turns/prompts before the abc-conditional bound was first settled. (The scaffold continued running for long afterward in unsuccessful attempts to remove the abc dependence.) Here is ChatGPT-5.5-Pro verifying the solution: check 1, check 2, check 3.
comments.meta: DavidTurturean тАФ 22:15 on 03 May 2026 ЁЯСН 2 ЁЯУЭ 0 ЁЯдЦ 0

comments.text: Congrats. Standard check found no issues in the writeup.

comments.text: Some initial observations inspired by the numerical data. The obvious factorization $n! = 2 \dots n$ gives $f(n) \leq n-2$ for all $n>2$. It also seems that $f(n) = n-O(\log n)$ for all but a few exceptional $n$. Indeed, suppose that $m=f(n) < n - C \log n$ for a large $C$. By Kummer's theorem, $2$ divides $n!$ $n-O(\log n)$ times, and if $2^k$ is the largest power of $2$ that divides one of the $a_i$, then the number of powers of $2$ dividing $a_1 \dots a_t$ is at most $\sum_{j=1}^k \min( \frac{m}{2^j}+1, t)$, which one can bound by $m+k$ by ignoring the $t$ factor; thus $k > 10 \log n$ (say) by hypothesis, which makes at least one of the $a_i$ of size at least $2^k \geq n^{10}$ hence all of them are at least this size since $m \leq n$. By Stirling's formula this gives the bound $t k \ll n \log n$, so $t$ is less than a small multiple of $n$. The inequality $n - O(\log n) \leq \sum_{j=1}^k \min( \frac{m}{2^j}+1, t) \ll k + t \log \frac{m}{t}$ then forces $k \gg n$, and hence $t = O(\log n)$. Thus $n!$ has somehow been factored into only $O(\log n)$ factors that are distance $O(n)$ apart and size at least $n^{10}$, which seems like quite a rare occurrence. These factors would need to be divisible by a lot of small primes, and perhaps one could rule a lot of scenarios out using the abc conjecture or similar tools.
comments.meta: TerenceTao тАФ 18:56 on 16 Sep 2025 ЁЯСН 0 ЁЯУЭ 0 ЁЯдЦ 0

comments.text: To continue the previous analysis: suppose that one has $t = O(\log n)$ for some large $n$ as above. For any prime $p \leq n^{0.99}$, $p$ divides $n! = a_1 \dots a_t$ about $n/p$ times, but if $l > 10 \log n$ (say) then at most one of the $a_1,\dots,a_t$ can be divisible by $p^l$. From this and the assumption $t = O(\log n)$ one can conclude that one of the $a_i$ is divisible by $p$ at least $0.99 n/p$ times (say). The product $\prod_{p \leq n^{0.99}} p^{0.99 n/p}$ is something like $(n!)^{0.98}$, so by the pigeonhole principle one should be able to find two $a_i,a_j$ each of which is divisible by a product of prime powers $p^{0.99 n/p}$ of size $\gg a_i^{0.95}, a_j^{0.95}$ say, which makes the radical of $a_i$ or $a_j$ of size $O(a_i^{0.1})$ or $O(a_j^{0.1})$. But as $a_i = a_j + O(n)$ and $a_i, a_j \geq n^{10}$, this would violate the abc conjecture. So I think one can get a bound of the form $f(n) = n - O(\log n)$ assuming the abc conjecture. Another observation: if one can find some small $j,l$ such that $(j+1) \dots (j+l) | \binom{n+j}{j} = \frac{(j+1) \dots (j+n)}{n!}$, then this almost certainly leads to the bound $f(n) \leq n-l-1$, because $(j+l+1) \dots (j+n)$ is now a moderately large multiple of $n!$ and one should be able to remove some terms from this product to recover $n!$ exactly. Thus for instance the trivial $1 | \binom{n+0}{0}$ gives the trivial bound $f(n) \leq n-2$, $2 \times 3 | \binom{n+1}{1}$ or $3 \times 4 | \binom{n+2}{2}$ should lead to $f(n) \leq n-3$, and so forth.
comments.meta: TerenceTao тАФ 13:28 on 17 Sep 2025 ЁЯСН 0 ЁЯУЭ 0 ЁЯдЦ 0

comments.text: Perhaps this is similar to the argument of Luca [Lu02], which conditional on ABC shows that, for any fixed polynomial $P$, there are finitely many solutions to $P(x)=n!$ (which as noted in the problem description implies $f(n)\to \infty$). I had a quick look at Luca's paper and it's not written in a quantitative way, but the argument should be easily adapted to give quantitative control in this specific case; I suspect it would then end up being the same as the argument you sketch.
comments.meta: Thomas Bloom тАФ 13:53 on 17 Sep 2025 ЁЯСН 0 ЁЯУЭ 0 ЁЯдЦ 0

comments.text: Over at the database, swrlly has made an initial computation of the first few values of $f(n)$, up to $n=11$, and some upper bounds for $n$ up to $19$, via a simple brute-force method. If any computation-minded folks would like to try confirming and/or extending these values, perhaps using a more efficient approach, that would be great; then we can work on submitting this new sequence to the OEIS (it does not appear to already be there). p.s. the OEIS display code should be modified so that only strings of the form "Axxxxxx" are linked; the "in progress" string does not link to anything useful.
